summary: Gruber follows up on his earlier analysis of the iPhone Notes app's UI design, sharing reader feedback on two main issues. The first concerns sorting: a reader argues convincingly that sorting by modification date rather than creation date is disruptive, causing notes to jump around unpredictably when edited. Gruber concedes the point, noting creation-date sorting would better serve heavy users without affecting light users, and would better match the paper notebook metaphor. The second issue addresses Apple's 'dynamic default.png' trick, where apps save a screenshot on quit and display it on next launch to simulate instant loading. Gruber argues this technique is deceptive — it makes launch times look faster without feeling faster, and frustrates users who tap on what appears to be a loaded UI but is actually a static image.
key_points: Sorting notes by modification date breaks chronological ordering when old notes are edited, making large collections of notes nearly impossible to navigate
Creation-date sorting would better serve power users, wouldn't affect light users, and would be more consistent with the paper notebook visual metaphor
Apple's dynamic default.png trick — saving a screenshot on quit to display on next launch — is only available to Apple's own apps due to code-signing restrictions on third-party app bundles
Dynamic default.png files are counterproductive because they make apps look loaded when they aren't, frustrating users who try to interact with what is actually a static image
List views that fit exactly a whole number of rows on screen provide no visual indication that more content exists below, unlike rows that are partially cut off
money_quotes: I fully realize that the user's perception of performance is often more important than actual measured-by-a-stopwatch performance, but in the case of dynamic default.png files, I think it goes too far.
It is frustrating to see a complete UI that looks usable but isn't.
Dynamic default.png files make application launch times look faster, but they don't make them feel faster.
I feel like a dolt every time I get tricked into uselessly tapping UI elements on a default.png screen.

Switching Notes to creation date sorting seems like a good idea. It would work better for people like Raphael, who keep a large number of active notes — and it would work just about the same for people like me, with a small number of active notes.
Sorting by creation date would fit better with Notes's 'looks like a paper tablet' visual metaphor.
As soon as you see actual content, you know the app is ready to use.
key_insight: Good UI design requires honesty with the user — whether in how content is sorted or how loading states are presented, deceiving users about what's happening erodes trust even when the intent is to improve perceived performance.
